title: The Briny Grave
content:
آ آ آ آ You wonder why so many would be buried in the sea,
آ آ آ آ In this world of froth and bubble,
آ آ آ آ But I donï؟½t wonder, for it seems to me
آ آ آ آ That it saves such a lot of trouble.
آ آ آ آ And there ainï؟½t no undertaker,
آ آ آ آ Oh! there ainï؟½t no order that your friends can give
آ آ آ آ On the quiet to the coffin-maker,
آ آ آ آ To a gimcrack coffin-maker,
آ آ آ آ They make no differ twixt the absentee swell
آ آ آ آ And the clerk that cut from a ï؟½shortageï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Oh! there ainï؟½t no pauper funer-el,
آ آ آ آ And there ainï؟½t no ï؟½impressive cortege.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ It may be a chap from the forï؟½ard crowd,
آ آ آ آ Or a member of the British Peerage,
آ آ آ آ But they sew his nibs in a canvas shroud
آ آ آ آ Just the same as the bloke from the steerage,
آ آ آ آ As that poor bloke from the steerage.
آ آ آ آ There ainï؟½t no need for a gravedigger there,
آ آ آ آ For you dig your own grave! Lord love yer!
آ آ آ آ And there ainï؟½t no use for a headstone fair
آ آ آ آ When the waters close above yer!
آ آ آ آ The little headstone where they come to weep,
آ آ آ آ May be right for the landï؟½s dry-rotters,
آ آ آ آ But you rest just as sound when youï؟½re anchored deep
آ آ آ آ With the pigiron at your trotters,
آ آ آ آ (Our fathers had iron at their trotters).
آ آ آ آ The sea is democratic the wide world round,
آ آ آ آ And it donï؟½t give a hang for no man,
آ آ آ آ There ainï؟½t no Church of England burial ground,
آ آ آ آ Nor yet there ainï؟½t no Roman.
آ آ آ آ Orthodox and hetï؟½rodox by wreck-strewn cliffs,
آ آ آ آ At peace in the stormiest weather,
آ آ آ آ Might bob up and down like two brother ï؟½stiffs,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And rest in one shark together,
آ آ آ آ And mix up their bones together.

آ آ آ آ The bare-headed skipper is as good any day
آ آ آ آ As an authorised shifter of sin is,
آ آ آ آ And the tear of shipmate is better anyway
آ آ آ آ Than the tear of the next-of-kin is.
آ آ آ آ It saves your friends, and it fills your needs,
آ آ آ آ It is best when all is reckoned,
آ آ آ آ And she canï؟½t come there in her widder weeds,
آ آ آ آ With her eyes on a likely second,
آ آ آ آ And a spot for the likely second.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
